findings.description: Overall page layout is not preserved; the HTML converts a single full-page map composition into a stacked report layout.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the page is dominated by one large map with title, legend, notes, scale, and figure metadata embedded around the map frame. In the HTML, the map is reduced to a small image at the top, followed by separately stacked title text, bullet list, sample IDs, tables, and notes.

findings.description: Map graphic is present but substantially reduced and loses functional legibility.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows a large readable map with labeled sample points, callout boxes, legend, north arrow, and site boundaries. In the HTML, the same map appears as a much smaller thumbnail, making internal labels and callouts difficult or impossible to read directly from the image.

findings.description: Text styling and emphasis are only partially preserved.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses embedded callout boxes, vertical side title text, and map-integrated labels. The HTML converts these into plain section headings, bullets, and simple tables, preserving content but losing much of the original visual hierarchy and integrated formatting.

findings.description: The HTML introduces fabricated title-block text not present verbatim in the PDF body as extractable content, likely inferred from the map border and labels rather than faithfully transcribed document structure.
findings.evidence: HTML adds a standalone section with headings and paragraphs: "SURFACE WATER ASSESSMENT", "SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL", "NCDEQ ID NO. NONCD00000870 TASK ORDER 870-RI-15", "REMOUNT ROAD, CHARLOTTE, MECKLENBURG COUNTY NC", "SCALE: 1\": 200'", "DATE: 10/28/2025", "PROJECT NUMBER: 215952", "FIGURE NO. 6". In the PDF, these appear as marginal/title-block graphics integrated into the map layout, not as a distinct structured text section.

findings.description: At least one table header/sample identifier is transcribed incorrectly, changing the underlying table data.
findings.evidence: HTML first table header: "SW-1/DUP-6102025-SD". PDF callout box reads: "SW-1/DUP-6102025-SD"? The map image appears to show a more complex identifier and the OCR/transcription is uncertain; because this is a sample ID, any mismatch is material. Also HTML converts map callout boxes into normalized tables, which may lose exact formatting and labeling relationships.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy does not reflect the source document structure and nests title-block map labels as semantic headings.
findings.evidence: HTML uses h1 for "SURFACE WATER ASSESSMENT", h2 for "SOUTHSIDE PARK LANDFILL", and h3 for "NOTES:" and "REFERENCE:". The PDF is a single map figure with border labels and callouts, not a multi-section narrative document. Converting map annotations into document headings creates an artificial hierarchy.

findings.description: Some alt text is generic and not sufficiently descriptive.
findings.evidence: HTML image alt attributes include "Map marker" and "Company Logo". These are generic and do not describe the actual symbol/logo content. The main map alt text "Map of Southside Park Landfill showing surface water assessment locations" is reasonable.

findings.description: The HTML contains duplicated structural wrapper elements and repeated figure nesting not present in the PDF.
findings.evidence:
HTML has nested
and repeated nested constructs. While not text duplication, this is duplicated structure introduced during conversion and does not correspond to the PDF layout.

findings.description: Some extracted text appears inaccurate or altered, especially in fine-print notes/reference lines and sample labels.
findings.evidence: HTML reference text includes "Geodatastyrelsen" and long GIS source strings that may be OCR-normalized, but the PDF fine print is difficult and includes punctuation/capitalization that are not preserved exactly. HTML also states "SURFACE WATER SAMPLE RESULTS THAT EXCEEDED THE 15A NCAC 2B GROUNDWATER STANDARDS ARE SHOWN," which may reflect a transcription inconsistency because the figure is for surface water assessment and exact regulatory wording in the PDF should be preserved verbatim.
